facts.label: PLAVI TO ORLOVI
facts.story: For 86 years, from the 1920s until 2006, the national team wore blue and carried the nickname "Plavi" — the Blues. When Montenegro split from Serbia that year, the federation switched to a red shirt with blue shorts and white socks, honoring the Serbian tricolor, and the crest gained a white double-headed eagle. Fans renamed the side "Orlovi," the Eagles. In 2025, nineteen years later, Serbia brought blue back into the kit, reviving the old nickname alongside the new one.

facts.label: LOS ICHACHOS
facts.story: At the inaugural 1930 World Cup in Montevideo, Yugoslavia fielded the youngest squad in the tournament, averaging barely 22 years old, yet opened with a stunning 2–1 win over Brazil at Parque Central. The scrappy, fast-attacking teenagers charmed the host country's crowds so much that Uruguayan fans gave them a nickname of their own: "Los Ichachos." The affection didn't save them from a 6–1 semi-final beating at the hands of the tournament hosts, but the nickname outlived the result.

1930.headline: Teenagers Topple Brazil
1930.story: Yugoslavia's squad, the youngest at the inaugural tournament with an average age near 22, opened with a stunning 2–1 win over Brazil in Montevideo behind goals from Aleksandar Tirnanić and Ivan Bek. A 4–0 win over Bolivia followed. Host Uruguay ended the run 6–1 in the semi-final, but Uruguayan fans had already nicknamed them 'Los Ichachos.'

annotations.label: THE VANISHING GOAL
annotations.story: Down 3–1 to Uruguay in the semi-final, Yugoslavia had a goal waved off just before halftime on a tight offside call, and one account has a policeman kicking a stray ball back into play to help set up a Uruguayan strike. Furious, Yugoslavia refused to contest the third-place match entirely.

annotations.label: NO LOGO, NO SPONSOR
annotations.story: The dark blue jersey worn in Montevideo carried no manufacturer's mark of any kind — Yugoslavia's kits stayed entirely in-house, stitched without a single brand stripe or crest sponsor, for 38 more years. Not until 1968 would a commercial kit maker's name ever touch the shirt.

annotations.label: THREE WEEKS BY SEA
annotations.story: With the passenger liner Conte Verde already booked solid by France, Belgium, and Romania, Yugoslavia's squad took a three-day train to Marseille and boarded the pleasure cruiser Florida alone. Egypt was meant to join them; their own ship was delayed by weather and the Florida sailed without them.

1930.homeColors: dark blue shirt, white shorts, red socks

1950.headline: Denied By Rio Heartbreak
1950.story: Yugoslavia thrashed Switzerland 3–0 and Mexico 4–1 to open Brazil's tournament, needing just a draw against the hosts to reach the final round-robin. Brazil beat them 2–0 in Rio de Janeiro instead, eliminating Yugoslavia on goal difference in one of the tightest group finishes of the era.

annotations.label: TEN MEN IN RIO
annotations.story: Captain Rajko Mitić gashed his head on a low iron beam leaving the tunnel before kickoff against Brazil and had to be stitched up pitchside. The referee refused to delay kickoff, so Yugoslavia played the opening minutes with ten men and never fully recovered, losing 2–0.

annotations.label: THE COLORS THAT SURVIVED
annotations.story: Twenty years after the team's first match, the same dark blue shirt, white shorts, and red socks took the field in Brazil — unchanged through a world war, a Nazi occupation, and the collapse of the monarchy into a socialist republic under Tito.

annotations.label: BREAKING FROM MOSCOW
annotations.story: Two years after Stalin's Cominform expelled Yugoslavia over Tito's refusal to bow to Soviet control, triggering a crushing economic embargo, Yugoslavia was the only Eastern Bloc nation to show up in Brazil — the USSR, Hungary, and Czechoslovakia all stayed home.

1950.homeColors: dark blue shirt, white shorts, red socks

1954.headline: Halted By The Miracle Men
1954.story: Miloš Milutinović's goal beat France, and Branko Zebec's equalizer salvaged a 1–1 draw with Brazil to send Yugoslavia into the quarter-finals in Switzerland. West Germany, riding the momentum that would carry them to the 'Miracle of Bern,' shut Yugoslavia out 2–0 and ended their run.

annotations.label: MILUTINOVIĆ'S OPENER
annotations.story: Miloš Milutinović's 15th-minute strike was enough to sink France in Lausanne, and Branko Zebec's second-half leveler against tournament favorites Brazil held Yugoslavia to a 1–1 draw — a rare point taken off the eventual runners-up before West Germany's defense shut them out in the quarter-final.

annotations.label: TWO DECADES BEFORE A LOGO
annotations.story: The navy shirt worn in Switzerland was still made in-house, with two more decades to pass before Yugoslavia wore its first branded manufacturer's kit in 1974. No stripes, no swoosh — just plain wool and a crest, unchanged since the team's founding.

annotations.label: NINE YEARS AFTER LIBERATION
annotations.story: Less than a decade removed from Nazi occupation and a brutal partisan resistance, Tito's rebuilding Yugoslavia sent a team to Switzerland as a statement of national recovery — a socialist state charting its own path, distinct from Moscow, on football's biggest stage.

1954.homeColors: dark blue shirt, white shorts, red socks

1958.headline: Petaković Can't Break Germany
1958.story: Aleksandar Petaković scored in three straight matches as Yugoslavia scraped through a tricky group with Scotland, France, and Paraguay. West Germany's defense proved the difference in the quarter-final, though, with Yugoslavia falling 1–0 despite a spirited showing in Sweden.

annotations.label: PETAKOVIĆ'S SCORING STREAK
annotations.story: Aleksandar Petaković found the net in three consecutive matches, dragging Yugoslavia through a bruising group against Scotland, France, and Paraguay that finished with two draws and a win. West Germany's back line proved the one defense he couldn't crack, shutting him out 1–0 in the quarter-final.

annotations.label: THE PLAIN WHITE CHANGE
annotations.story: Yugoslavia's away kit in Sweden was a plain white long-sleeved jersey with a V-neck collar, still made in-house, with a multicolored crest stitched prominently on the left chest — the only splash of color on an otherwise stripped-down, unbranded shirt.

annotations.label: A BRIDGE ACROSS BLOCS
annotations.story: Tito's independent socialist line kept Yugoslavia diplomatically useful to both Cold War camps, and its footballers reflected that: a communist-governed side sharing a group with Scotland and France, comfortably embedded in a Western European tournament calendar that shut out most of the Eastern Bloc.

1958.homeColors: dark blue shirt, white shorts, red socks

1962.headline: Jerković's Golden Boot Run
1962.story: Dražan Jerković's four goals carried Yugoslavia past Uruguay and Colombia, then Milan Radaković's strike shocked West Germany 1–0 in the quarter-final in Chile. Czechoslovakia ended the run in the semi-final, and a weary Yugoslavia lost the third-place match to the hosts, settling for fourth again.

annotations.label: JERKOVIĆ'S GOLDEN RUN
annotations.story: Dražan Jerković scored four times to fire Yugoslavia past Uruguay and Colombia, and Milan Radaković's 85th-minute strike stunned West Germany in the quarter-final. Czechoslovakia ended the run in the semi, and a spent Yugoslavia lost the third-place match to hosts Chile, settling for fourth again.

annotations.label: THE TRICOLOR CREST
annotations.story: Yugoslavia's away kit in Chile was a plain white jersey with a V-neck collar, still stitched in-house with no manufacturer's name, but its left-chest crest burst with red, yellow, and blue — the only vivid detail on an otherwise minimalist shirt.

annotations.label: A YEAR AFTER BELGRADE
annotations.story: Just months after Belgrade hosted the founding summit of the Non-Aligned Movement in 1961, Tito's Yugoslavia arrived in South America positioning itself as a bridge between capitalist and communist blocs — a diplomatic balancing act mirrored by a football team that beat both West Germany and Uruguay.

1962.homeColors: dark blue shirt, white shorts, red socks

1974.headline: Nine Unanswered Goals
1974.story: Dušan Bajević's hat-trick headlined a 9–0 demolition of Zaire, still the biggest win in the team's World Cup history, after a scoreless draw with Brazil. West Germany, Poland, and Sweden then swept the second group stage in West Germany, ending a promising run just short of the semis.

annotations.label: NINE GOALS, ONE RECORD
annotations.story: Dušan Bajević's hat-trick anchored a 9–0 demolition of Zaire that stands as the biggest win in the program's World Cup history, a rout with nine different sequences finding the net after a scoreless draw with Brazil. A stacked second group with West Germany, Poland, and Sweden ended the run just short of the semis.

annotations.label: THE FIRST THREE STRIPES
annotations.story: After 44 years of unbranded, in-house jerseys, 1974 marked Yugoslavia's first-ever manufacturer kit: an Adidas shirt with the German brand's three stripes trimming the sleeves, joining the era's dominant football supplier for the first time in the team's history.

annotations.label: BROTHERHOOD ON THE PITCH
annotations.story: The 1974 squad drew players from across Yugoslavia's constituent socialist republics, a multi-ethnic roster fielded at the high-water mark of Tito's 'Brotherhood and Unity' era — decades before the country's ethnic divisions would tear both the nation and its football apart.

1974.homeColors: dark blue shirt, white shorts, red socks

matches.scorers: Bajević 8', Džajić 14', Šurjak 18', Katalinski 22', Bajević 30', Bogićević 35', Oblak 61', Petković 65', Bajević 81'

1982.headline: A Quiet Group Stage Exit
1982.story: A scoreless draw with Northern Ireland opened Yugoslavia's tournament in Spain, and Petar Gudelj's strike against the hosts wasn't enough in a 2–1 loss. Petrović's goal beat Honduras to close the group, but goal difference eliminated Yugoslavia just short of the second round.

annotations.label: GUDELJ'S LONE STRIKE
annotations.story: Petar Gudelj's goal against host nation Spain wasn't enough to prevent a 2–1 loss, and a scoreless opener against Northern Ireland left little margin. Petrović's late winner beat Honduras to close the group, but the numbers didn't add up — Yugoslavia went out on goal difference.

annotations.label: PINSTRIPES AND THREE STRIPES
annotations.story: Yugoslavia's Adidas away kit in Spain was a white shirt threaded with thin blue pinstripes, a classic collar, and red-and-blue Adidas stripes across the shoulders — a subtler design than the plain navy home shirt the team had worn for half a century.

annotations.label: PLAYING IN MOURNING
annotations.story: This was the first World Cup Yugoslavia entered after Josip Broz Tito's death in May 1980, the leader whose decades-long grip had held the country's six republics together. The quiet group-stage exit came as the nation he built began, almost imperceptibly, to drift toward the fractures of the following decade.

1982.homeColors: dark blue shirt, white shorts, red socks

1990.headline: Stojković Stuns Spain
1990.story: Dragan Stojković scored twice to beat Spain 2–1 in extra time in the Round of 16, sending Yugoslavia into a quarter-final showdown with Diego Maradona's Argentina. Neither side scored through 120 minutes in Florence, and Argentina survived the shootout 3–2, ending one of Yugoslavia's best modern runs.

annotations.label: STOJKOVIĆ'S EXTRA-TIME DOUBLE
annotations.story: Dragan Stojković scored in the 78th and 93rd minutes to sink Spain 2–1 in extra time, sending Yugoslavia into a quarter-final against Diego Maradona's Argentina. Neither side broke through in 120 goalless minutes in Florence, and Argentina survived the shootout 3–2 to end the run.

annotations.label: THE LIGHTNING BOLT SHIRT
annotations.story: Yugoslavia's Adidas away kit that summer was a white shirt slashed with red-and-blue lightning-bolt graphics down the sides and a blue V-neck collar — a bold departure from the plain navy home strip the team had worn largely unchanged since 1930.

annotations.label: THE BOBAN ABSENCE
annotations.story: Weeks before the tournament, Dinamo Zagreb captain Zvonimir Boban kicked a policeman during the Maksimir riot between Dinamo and Red Star fans, an incident tied to Croatia's independence movement. His six-month ban kept him off the 1990 squad — a preview of the breakup to come.

1990.homeColors: dark blue shirt, white shorts, red socks

1998.headline: Komljenović's Breakthrough Run
1998.story: Slobodan Komljenović scored the winner against the United States and struck again as the Netherlands eliminated FR Yugoslavia 2–1 in the Round of 16. Predrag Mijatović and Dragan Stojković had rescued a 2–2 draw with Germany in the group, but the Dutch ended the run in France.

annotations.label: KOMLJENOVIĆ'S BREAKTHROUGH
annotations.story: Slobodan Komljenović's solitary strike beat the United States, and he found the net again against the Netherlands in the Round of 16 — but Edgar Davids's 92nd-minute winner sent the Dutch through 2–1. Predrag Mijatović and Dragan Stojković had earlier salvaged a 2–2 draw with Germany.

annotations.label: ONE CENTRAL STRIPE
annotations.story: The Adidas away shirt worn in France was mostly white with a single dark navy stripe running down the center, a V-neck trimmed in blue, white, and red, and matching three stripes on the shoulders — a design pared back from the loud graphics of 1990.

annotations.label: THE LOST GENERATION'S REUNION
annotations.story: UN sanctions had expelled Yugoslavia from all international football in 1992 over the Bosnian war, wiping out an entire golden generation that never played Euro 92 or USA 94. This was that generation's only real World Cup — reassembled under a new flag, a rump federation of just Serbia and Montenegro.

1998.homeColors: dark blue shirt, white shorts, red socks

2006.headline: Argentina's Six-Goal Humiliation
2006.story: Serbia and Montenegro's farewell World Cup turned disastrous in Germany: a 1–0 loss to the Netherlands, then a 6–0 rout by Argentina, still the program's worst World Cup defeat. Nikola Žigić and Ilić scored consolation goals in a 2–3 loss to Ivory Coast to close a winless tournament.

annotations.label: THE WORST DEFEAT ON RECORD
annotations.story: A 1–0 opening loss to the Netherlands was merely a warm-up for what followed: a 6–0 demolition by Argentina that remains the heaviest defeat in the program's World Cup history. Žigić and Ilić's consolation goals in a 2–3 finale loss to Ivory Coast closed out a winless tournament.

annotations.label: LOTTO'S FAREWELL KIT
annotations.story: Made by Italian manufacturer Lotto, this shirt would be the last one ever to carry the name 'Serbia and Montenegro' — a jersey representing a federation that, by the time the tournament kicked off, had already voted itself out of existence.

annotations.label: A COUNTRY THAT NO LONGER EXISTED
annotations.story: Montenegro's independence referendum passed on May 21, 2006, just over two weeks before the tournament began. Serbia and Montenegro's players took the field in Germany representing a state that had legally dissolved before their first match — the last gasp of a union already over.

2006.homeColors: dark blue shirt, white shorts, red socks

2010.headline: Jovanović Topples Germany
2010.story: Milan Jovanović's strike shocked defending finalists Germany 1–0 in South Africa, one of the tournament's biggest upsets. But an opening loss to Ghana and Pantelić's consolation goal in a 2–1 defeat to Australia left Serbia eliminated on goal difference despite beating the runners-up.

annotations.label: JOVANOVIĆ'S SHOCK WINNER
annotations.story: Milan Jovanović's 38th-minute strike downed defending runners-up Germany 1–0, one of the biggest upsets of the tournament. It wasn't enough — an opening loss to Ghana and a 2–1 defeat to Australia, with only a Pantelić consolation goal, sent Serbia out on goal difference anyway.

annotations.label: THE FIRST TRUE RED SHIRT
annotations.story: This was Serbia's first World Cup as a standalone nation, and the Nike-made kit reflected it: a vibrant red shirt with a bold white cross design and subtle blue trim, replacing the century-old dark blue jersey the team had worn as Yugoslavia and Serbia and Montenegro.

annotations.label: A FLAG STILL BEING DRAWN
annotations.story: Serbia arrived in South Africa as its own country for the first time since Montenegro's split four years earlier, while Kosovo's 2008 declaration of independence remained unrecognized by Belgrade — the borders of the former Yugoslavia still being contested even as this new red shirt made its debut.

2010.homeColors: red shirt, blue shorts, white socks

2018.headline: Kolarov's Free Kick Opener
2018.story: Aleksandar Kolarov's free kick beat Costa Rica in Serbia's opener, but Xherdan Shaqiri's late winner sent Switzerland to a 2–1 comeback win, and Brazil closed out Serbia 2–0 in Moscow. A promising start faded fast in a tightly bunched Group E.
2018.homeColors: red shirt, blue shorts, white socks

2022.headline: Mitrović's Late Cameroon Rescue
2022.story: Aleksandar Mitrović's stoppage-time header salvaged a wild 3–3 draw with Cameroon after Richarlison had sunk Serbia against Brazil. Xherdan Shaqiri's Switzerland struck again in a 2–3 finale loss, and Serbia went out on goal difference for the third straight tournament.
2022.homeColors: red shirt, blue shorts, white socks
